title: Multidimensional arrays

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Multidimensional arrays; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Multidimensional arrays
sources.claim: A multidimensional C array is an array of arrays stored in row-major order, and pointer arithmetic requires the inner dimensions to determine each row's stride.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A multidimensional C array is an array of arrays stored in row-major order, and pointer arithmetic requires the inner dimensions to determine each row's stride.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An int grid[2][3] stores three contiguous int elements in each of two contiguous rows, and grid[r][c] selects row then column. Your goal is to implement a bounded two-dimensional grid traversal and explain its layout. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: while, do and for; Parameters and return values; One-dimensional arrays. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A multidimensional C array is an array of arrays stored in row-major order, and pointer arithmetic requires the inner dimensions to determine each row's stride. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Variable-length arrays are optional in some later implementations, dynamically allocated flat buffers use a different type, and irregular rows require pointer-based structures. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: A pointer to int can always be used as an int two-dimensional array without carrying or computing row stride.

includes: Use row-major nested arrays and pass dimensions correctly to functions.
Implement a bounded two-dimensional grid traversal and explain its layout.
excludes: Variable-length arrays are optional in some later implementations, dynamically allocated flat buffers use a different type, and irregular rows require pointer-based structures.

outcomes: Implement a bounded two-dimensional grid traversal and explain its layout.

examples.title: Multidimensional arrays: defined case
examples.situation: An int grid[2][3] stores three contiguous int elements in each of two contiguous rows, and grid[r][c] selects row then column.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: An int grid[2][3] stores three contiguous int elements in each of two contiguous rows, and grid[r][c] selects row then column.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Preserve the inner dimension in the pointer type or flatten deliberately with an explicit row-stride calculation.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Preserve the inner dimension in the pointer type or flatten deliberately with an explicit row-stride calculation.

examples.title: Multidimensional arrays: boundary case
examples.situation: A function receiving a 2D array declares the column bound so advancing one row skips exactly that many elements.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A function receiving a 2D array declares the column bound so advancing one row skips exactly that many elements.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Variable-length arrays are optional in some later implementations, dynamically allocated flat buffers use a different type, and irregular rows require pointer-based structures.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: A pointer to int can always be used as an int two-dimensional array without carrying or computing row stride.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement a bounded two-dimensional grid traversal and explain its layout.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A pointer to int can always be used as an int two-dimensional array without carrying or computing row stride.
misconceptions.correction: Preserve the inner dimension in the pointer type or flatten deliberately with an explicit row-stride calculation.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: A pointer to int can always be used as an int two-dimensional array without carrying or computing row stride.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Multidimensional arrays: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Variable-length arrays are optional in some later implementations, dynamically allocated flat buffers use a different type, and irregular rows require pointer-based structures.
